serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of herbal tea (approximately 240 ml) is a typical single serving for an adult.

nutrient_source.title: Herbal Tea — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for herbal tea (sweetened), food subgroup: Asian and herbal drinks. All per-100g values preserved from the input record which carries HPB provenance at 0.95 confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweetened herbal tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the analogous food 'sugar-sweetened beverage / fruit drink' category from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 13.9) / 100 ≈ 7.2 for a 240 g serving (13.9 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.6 g sugar per 100 ml; sodium is negligible at 1 mg per 100 ml
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is negligible (1 mg/100 ml), the sugar content of 5.6 g/100 ml classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB); AHA 2018 guidelines link regular SSB consumption to elevated blood pressure, so individuals with hypertension should choose the unsweetened version.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (5.6 g sugar/100 ml, zero dietary fibre); liquid sugar raises postprandial glucose rapidly
type2_diabetes.guidance: This sweetened herbal tea qualifies as an SSB with 5.6 g sugar per 100 ml and zero fibre; liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly and cause sharp postprandial glucose excursions, making this unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes — the unsweetened version is strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar from SSB classification; fat and sodium are negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides and cardiovascular risk per WHO 2015 free-sugar guidelines; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this sweetened herbal tea and opt for the unsweetened variant.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no confirmed anti-inflammatory active compounds present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The added sugar in this sweetened herbal tea may promote inflammatory mediators relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; switching to an unsweetened herbal tea with confirmed polyphenol content is recommended for individuals with RA.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Herbal tea (also called tisane or herbal infusion) is a caffeine-free beverage brewed from dried herbs, flowers, fruits, or botanical ingredients steeped in hot water. Unlike true teas derived from Camellia sinensis, herbal teas contain no tea leaves and are naturally caffeine-free. This sweetened commercial variant, as recorded in the Singapore HPB Foodo database, contains 5.79 g carbohydrate and 5.6 g sugar per 100 ml, contributing 23 kcal per 100 ml. Protein and fat are naturally zero as this is a plant-infusion beverage with no protein-containing or fat-containing ingredients. Dietary fibre is zero in the liquid form. Sodium is negligible at 1 mg per 100 ml. The beverage provides trace calcium (2 mg) and iron (0.08 mg) per 100 ml. Clinically, the sugar content classifies this product as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under WHO and AHA definitions (>5 g sugar per 100 ml beverage), which raises concern for type 2 diabetes management, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The unsweetened version of herbal tea is a suitable low-calorie, caffeine-free alternative for individuals managing these conditions. Herbal teas are widely consumed across Asia and globally as a health-oriented beverage, often associated with relaxation, digestive support, and antioxidant intake from polyphenols in the botanical ingredients.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with 0.95 confidence as recorded in the input. The food is a well-defined commercial beverage category with exact HPB provenance. GI is estimated from an analogous SSB category in the University of Sydney GI Database, which reduces GI confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong macro provenance.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero as herbal tea is a plant-infusion beverage containing no protein-contributing ingredients. fat_g is naturally zero as herbal tea contains no fat-contributing ingredients — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero in the liquid beverage form per HPB. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero consistent with a plant-based beverage. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this herbal tea entry are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened beverage / fruit drink category from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for sweetened herbal tea was found in the approved GI source. This record represents the sweetened commercial variant of herbal tea as sold in Singapore; an unsweetened variant would have substantially different nutritional and clinical profiles. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03301103 and FoodEx2 A0FJP are defensible matches for herbal infusion; SNOMED CT 229953004 is used as an exact match for herbal tea substance.
